content:
17 min read
Just now

--

In June 2026 I sat down and added up what helpdesk software was going to cost me as my product grew. The number I got was uncomfortable enough that I decided to solve it like an engineering problem instead of a billing problem. This post is everything that followed the real pricing across every tool in the market, the architecture I designed, every technical decision I made, every wall I hit, and exactly how the money worked out.
If you run a product with existing users and you’re about to plug in a support tool, this is what I wish someone had handed me before I started
The Full Pricing Picture Every Major Tool, Honestly The number they show you is not the number you pay
Every helpdesk pricing page has a “starts at” number that is technically accurate and practically misleading. The low number is almost always the annual billing rate, which requires committing to a 12 month contract upfront.
All prices below are monthly billing rates.
1 . Zoho Desk : The most affordable credible SaaS option
Zoho Desk has a permanent free plan for up to 3 agents, which is genuinely useful for getting started. When you outgrow it, the pricing is the most honest in the category.
Express: $9 per agent per month (limited to a maximum of 5 agents)
Standard: $20 per agent per month
Professional: $35 per agent per month
Enterprise: $50 per agent per month
Express capped at 5 agents is an important limit. If you have more than 5 people answering tickets, you jump directly to Standard at $20 per agent. For a team of 6, that is $120 per month versus $54 on Express. Worth knowing.
2 . Freshdesk : A free tier with an expiry date
Freshdesk has a free tier for up to 2 agents but it expires after 6 months. It exists to get you into the product, not to serve you long-term.
Growth: approximately $23 per agent per month (monthly billing; annual rate is $19)
Pro: approximately $66 per agent per month (annual rate is $55)
Enterprise: approximately $107 per agent per month (annual rate is $89)
Freshdesk also offers Omni, a separate product that bundles voice, chat, email, and social in a unified view. Omni starts at $29 per agent per month on annual billing.
Freshdesk’s AI (Freddy) includes 500 free sessions per month on paid plans, with additional sessions at $49 per 100. As you push more conversations through AI, this becomes another line on your bill.
3 . Help Scout : Designed for quality, priced for it
Help Scout has a permanent free tier for up to 5 users but caps you at 100 contacts per billing cycle the moment the 101st person reaches out, replies stop until the cycle resets. It is a hard wall.
Standard: $25 per user per month
Plus: $50 per user per month (annual rate is $45)
Pro: $75 per user per month, with a minimum of 10 users (annual rate is $65)
The software is genuinely excellent for email heavy support. Clean interface, solid automation, thoughtful AI assist. The trade off is that features are gated heavily WhatsApp is Plus only, Salesforce and HubSpot integrations are Plus only, SSO is Pro-only. You pay for each tier of capability.
An AI Answers add on (autonomous resolution bot) is available at $0.75 per successful resolution, on top of the seat cost.
4 . Zendesk : The enterprise benchmark, priced accordingly
Zendesk has no free tier. You get a 14 day trial of the Suite Professional plan and then you choose.
Support Team (ticketing only, no live chat): $25 per agent per month (annual rate is $19)
Suite Team (ticketing plus live chat and messaging): $69 per agent per month (annual rate is $55)
Suite Professional: $149 per agent per month (annual rate is $115)
The jump from Support Team to Suite Team is the one most products make you need live chat. That jump is from $25 to $69 per agent per month, a 176% increase.
Zendesk also sells AI resolution credits (Fin) separately at $1.50 per resolved conversation on committed plans. As your AI deflection rate grows, this is an additional cost on top of every seat.
For 5 agents on Suite Team, billed monthly: $345.
5 . Crisp : Not per-agent: per workspace
6 . Front : Collaboration first, not support first
7 . Groove (now Helply) : A story in transition
The Cost With 5 Agents : Where the Gap Becomes Visible
> Self-hosted Frappe Helpdesk on a VPS: $12 per month. Fixed.
> Zoho Desk Express: $45 per month.
> Freshdesk Growth: $115 per month.
> Help Scout Standard: $125 per month.
> Freshdesk Pro: $330 per month.
> Zendesk Suite Team: $345 per month.
Every SaaS option scales linearly with headcount. Hire one more person to answer tickets and the number goes up immediately. The moment you promote someone part time into support, the number goes up. The seat model is a direct tax on the size of your support function.
The 12 Month Compounding Effect
The self hosted line includes a one time setup cost approximately 10 engineering hours to build and wire the proxy layer. That is front loaded into month 1. After that, the line is flat at $12 per month, every month, regardless of how many tickets come in, how many users you onboard, or how many agents you have answering questions.
After 12 months:
Self-hosted total: $944 (setup + 12 months of infrastructure)
Zoho Desk Express (5 agents): $540
Freshdesk Growth (5 agents): $1,380
Zendesk Suite Team (5 agents): $4,140
Intercom Advanced (5 agents): $5,940
Zoho Express actually beats self hosting in pure cash on a 12 month horizon at 5 agents. The engineering investment of 10 hours is the gap. If your team values engineering time at $80 per hour, the setup cost is $800 and you break even against Zoho Express around month 24. Against Zendesk, breakeven is month 4.
This is the calculation worth doing honestly for your own situation.
The Scaling Problem Why This Gets Worse as You Grow
The green line does not move. Self hosted infrastructure cost is the same whether you have 1 agent or 10. The VPS running Frappe does not get a bill for each new person who logs into the agent portal.
Every SaaS line goes up in direct proportion to headcount. You are not paying for the software’s capability you are paying for how many humans you employ to use it. Those are different things.
The Annual Savings : Concrete Numbers
At 1 agent: save $676 per year vs Zendesk Suite Team.
At 3 agents: save $2,364 per year.
At 5 agents: save $3,996 per year.
At 10 agents: save $8,136 per year.
Every year. These are not one time numbers. The savings compound as long as the self hosted system runs.
The Problem That Made Me Build This
I could have used Zoho Desk. The $9/agent Express tier is honest. The software is solid. But every one of these tools from Zoho to Zendesk shares one assumption that broke my specific situation.
The assumption: the customer needs an account in the helpdesk and must log into it to raise or track a ticket
My users already existed inside my app. They were already signed in. I had their names, their identifiers, their contact details, all of it, already. Asking them to create a second account, remember a second password, and navigate to a different domain just to ask a question would have shattered the experience I had spent months building.
The support screen has to feel like another page inside your product. Not a detour to someone else’s software.
And the shortcut calling the helpdesk API directly from the browser was not a shortcut at all. That requires shipping the API secret to the frontend, where anyone who opens DevTools can read it. With that key, they could read every ticket from every user in the system. That is not a configuration choice. That is a data breach waiting to be filed.
So the requirements were:
1 . Users authenticate with credentials my app already owns. No new account. No second login.
2 . The API secret lives on the server and never leaves it.
3 . Each user sees only their own tickets, enforced server side on every request, never trusting the browser.
4 . No per-seat billing.
Nothing on the market satisfied all four. So I built the layer that makes it possible.
The Architecture: One Thin Layer Between Your Users and the Helpdesk
The pattern has a name: Backend For Frontend, or BFF. A small server you control sits between the browser and the helpdesk. The browser talks to your server. Your server talks to the helpdesk using a service account key that stays on the server.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

ArchitectureThree guarantees fall out of this single architectural decision.
Identity is yours. The proxy authenticates users against your own data and issues your own session. The helpdesk is never consulted about who someone is.
The secret stays hidden. The Authorization header is assembled on the server from environment variables and never included in any response. The browser has no pathway to it.
Isolation is enforced. Every ticket endpoint derives “who is this” from the verified server side session and refuses anything outside that scope. The client cannot choose its own filter.
The helpdesk sees one trusted service account. The user sees one seamless product. Neither knows the other’s assumptions were bent.
The Engineering in Full Detail
Layer 1 : Passwords : Never Plain Text, Always Slow
Passwords are stored as scrypt hashes with per user salts
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why scrypt over bcrypt ? Both are fine. scrypt is memory hard an attacker trying to brute force your hash list needs memory as well as compute, which makes GPU parallelism expensive. It is also in Node’s built in crypto module with no extra dependency. The essential point is not which slow hash you pick it is that you pick a slow, salted hash, not MD5 or SHA-256 or anything that runs fast
Verification is constant time. This is not optional and it is not a minor hardening detail
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A normal string equality check short circuits the instant it finds a differing character. That timing difference measurable in microseconds, consistent enough to exploit at scale leaks information about how close a wrong password is to the correct one. Over enough attempts, this can be used to reconstruct a valid hash. timingSafeEqual always runs for the same duration. One line of code closes an entire class of attack.
Layer 2 : Sessions: Stateless, Signed, and Serverless
After login, every subsequent request needs to prove who the caller is without asking for their password again. There are two approaches.
Option A : Server-side sessions: a random session id is created, stored in a database or Redis, and given to the browser as a cookie. Every request does a lookup against the store.
Option B : Stateless signed tokens: the token contains the user id, signed with a secret, and given to the browser as a cookie. Every request verifies the signature if it holds, the contents are trusted. No database. No lookup. No Redis.
I used Option B. The token format is:
base64url(“user_id | timestamp”) joined with a period followed by HMAC-SHA256(payload, SECRET)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What you gain: no session store to maintain, replicate, or back up. Logins survive server restarts because the signing secret is stable. No Redis dependency for a simple setup.
What you accept: you cannot instantly invalidate a single session server-side. Revoking one user’s session requires either a deny list (a small table of invalidated token ids checked on each request) or rotating the signing secret, which logs everyone out simultaneously. For most internal tools and small to medium products the 7 day expiry is an acceptable trade. For anything that handles financial transactions or medical data, add the deny list from the start.
The cookie is HttpOnly : JavaScript in the page cannot read it and SameSite: lax so it is not sent on cross site requests.
Every protected route passes through one gate
Press enter or click to view image in full size

req.user is set once at the gate. Everything downstream reads from it. The browser’s request body is neve
